title_en: Chapter on the Excellence of Tawhid and the Sins It Expiates
title_ar: باب فضل التوحيد وما يكفّر من الذنوب
content_en:
Among the greatest motivators toward sincere Tawhid is the understanding of its immense reward and the extraordinary capacity it has to expiate sins. This chapter in Kitab al-Tawhid presents a series of Quranic verses and prophetic traditions that together illuminate the magnificent bounty that awaits those who die upon pure monotheism.

The primary hadith driving this chapter is the famous report from Ubadah ibn al-Samit, who narrated that the Prophet ﷺ said: 'Whoever testifies that there is no god worthy of worship except Allah alone, with no partner, and that Muhammad is His servant and Messenger, and that Isa is the servant of Allah and the son of His handmaid, and His Word which He cast into Maryam, and a spirit from Him, and that Paradise is real and Hellfire is real — Allah will admit him into Paradise regardless of his deeds.' This hadith, recorded by both al-Bukhari and Muslim, is a towering testament to the power of Tawhid when it is held with sincerity and conviction.

Another key text is the hadith narrated by Itban ibn Malik al-Ansari, in which the Prophet ﷺ declared: 'Indeed Allah has forbidden the Fire for one who says: There is no god worthy of worship except Allah, seeking thereby the Face of Allah.' This narration makes explicit the condition that must accompany the declaration of Tawhid — sincerity and exclusivity of purpose. Tawhid cannot be merely a verbal formula; it must arise from genuine conviction in the heart.

The expiation of sins through Tawhid is further illustrated by the well-known hadith in which the Prophet ﷺ described a man from among the later generations who will be brought before Allah on the Day of Judgment with ninety-nine scrolls of recorded sins, each scroll stretching as far as the eye can see. But because he possesses a card bearing the testimony of La ilaha illallah with sincerity, it will outweigh all of those scrolls on the scale. This hadith, recorded in the collections of al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Majah, is described by scholars as a source of great hope for the believer.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab draws an important distinction in this chapter between the theoretical understanding of Tawhid and its lived reality. A person may recite the Shahada their entire life while simultaneously committing acts of shirk — visiting graves with supplication, tying protective charms, seeking intercession from the dead — and thereby nullify the very protection and virtue that Tawhid would otherwise confer. The chapter therefore serves as both an encouragement and a warning: Tawhid offers incalculable spiritual benefit, but only when it is complete, sincere, and free from the blemish of associating partners with Allah.

The theological underpinning here is crucial: the expiation of sins through Tawhid is not a license for moral laxity but rather an acknowledgment that the acceptance of Allah's lordship is itself the greatest of all acts of worship. When a person's heart is sound in its Tawhid, the branches of righteousness naturally follow. The scholars of Ahl al-Sunnah have consistently held that Tawhid is not merely a starting point but the pervasive reality that gives meaning to all other acts of obedience.
